recent_facts.title: One Operator to Rule Them All
recent_facts.body:
From a recent paper that made mathematicians
do a double take:

Every elementary function you have ever used
— sin, cos, tan, exp, log, powers, roots,
hyperbolic functions, and even the constants
π and e — can ALL be generated from just
ONE binary operator and the number 1.

That operator is:
eml(x, y) = exp(x) − ln(y)

Think about what this means:
- sin(x)? Derivable from eml alone.
- π? Derivable from eml alone.
- x²? Derivable from eml alone.
- The entire landscape of elementary
  mathematics — one operator.

The CS analogy: NAND gates.
A single logic gate from which every
circuit, every processor, every computation
can be built. Mathematics just found its
NAND gate for elementary functions.

Important note: this does not mean
calculations become simpler. Composing
eml many times to recover sin(x) is
computationally expensive. But the
theoretical result is profound — the
entire zoo of elementary functions
is one family with one common ancestor.

🤔 What does this make you think about
the deeper structure of mathematics?

recent_facts.title: The Mathematician They Called a Nurse
recent_facts.body:
You know Florence Nightingale as the
founder of modern nursing. That is true
but deeply incomplete.

She was primarily a brilliant mathematician
and statistician who used data to save lives
at a time when almost no one trusted data.

During the Crimean War (1854-1856), she
collected meticulous records on soldier
deaths and discovered something shocking:
- Deaths from battle wounds: relatively few
- Deaths from preventable infections and
  poor sanitation: the vast majority

But no one believed her words. So she
invented an entirely new type of chart —
the Polar Area Diagram, now called the
Nightingale Rose Diagram — to make the
data impossible to ignore.

She presented it to British Parliament
and Queen Victoria. The visualization was
so compelling it directly led to sanitation
reforms that saved hundreds of thousands
of lives.

She was one of the first people in history
to use data visualization as a tool of
persuasion and policy change.

Today every research paper, every dashboard,
every data presentation owes something to
Florence Nightingale.

💡 Next time someone says your research
does not matter — remember her. 🌹

recent_facts.title: The Unreachable Number
recent_facts.body:
Using only the numbers 1, 2, 3 and the
operators + − × ÷ ^ what is the smallest
positive integer you CANNOT form?

Rules:
- Maximum 3 numbers total
- Maximum 2 operators
- Parentheses allowed
- No concatenation (1 and 2 cannot make 12)
- Numbers can repeat (2,2,3 is valid)

Examples of what IS possible:
✅ 7  = 2³ − 1
✅ 13 = 3² + 2²
✅ 17 = 3² + 2³
✅ 22 = 3³ − 3 − 2
✅ 29 = 3³ + 2

🤔 What is the first number you CANNOT reach?

Post your answer in the thread and
show your working for bonus respect! 👇

recent_facts.title: The Ball Transfer Problem
recent_facts.body:
Container A: 1,000 green + 3,000 red = 4,000 balls
Container B: 3,000 green + 1,000 red = 4,000 balls

Here is what happens:
1. Take exactly HALF the balls from A at random
2. Transfer them all to B
3. Pick ONE ball from B at random

🤔 What is the probability the picked
ball is green?

Most people's gut says 50%.
Are you most people?

Drop your answer in the discussion thread!

recent_facts.title: Only Legends Get It
recent_facts.body:
Each colored rectangle represents a day
of the week. The sizes are not random.

🤔 What mathematical pattern connects
the size of each day?

Drop your answer in the discussion thread!

recent_facts.title: Why Are You Still Typing on a 150-Year-Old Keyboard Layout?
recent_facts.body:
The keyboard you are using right now was
designed in 1873 — for a mechanical typewriter.

QWERTY was created by Christopher Latham Sholes
to solve a mechanical problem: commonly paired
letters were placed apart so physical metal
typebars would not jam together mid-sentence.

That problem has not existed since the 1980s.

The heatmap tells the story clearly:
- Red = most heavily used keys
- QWERTY constantly pulls fingers off home row
- Dvorak and Colemak place the 10 most common
  English letters directly under your fingers
- Studies show the average typist travels
  over 20km per day on QWERTY fingers

Fun detail: the word "TYPEWRITER" can be typed
entirely on the top row of QWERTY — almost
certainly not a coincidence for a salesman
demonstrating the machine in 1873.

🤔 Would you switch layouts if it meant
relearning to type for 3-4 weeks?

recent_facts.title: Can You Identify This Function?
recent_facts.body:
Look at this graph carefully before scrolling.

What you can observe:
- Symmetric about the y-axis
- Behaves like a parabola for large |x|
- Something very strange happens near x = 0
- Oscillations become infinitely rapid
  as you approach the origin

🤔 What function produces this graph?
Drop your answer in the discussion thread!

recent_facts.title: When Were You Born? Your Birthday's Popularity Rank
recent_facts.body:
Not all birthdays are created equal.

According to Australian Bureau of Statistics
data, birthdays are ranked from 1 (most common)
to 366 (least common — yes, including Feb 29).

Some surprising findings from the data:
- September dominates the top spots
- February 29 is the rarest (rank 366)
- Christmas Day and New Year's Day are
  among the LEAST common birthdays
- Most common birth months: September
  and October

🤔 Where does YOUR birthday rank?
Check the chart and drop your rank
in the reactions below!

Fun fact: If you were born in September,
your parents were likely celebrating the
holidays 9 months earlier... 🎄

recent_facts.title: The Coin Flip Trap
recent_facts.body:
Someone approaches you with a deal:

Flip 1: Heads → you win $1,000 | Tails → you pay $500
Flip 2: Heads → you win $2,000 | Tails → you pay $1,000
Flip 3: Heads → you win $4,000 | Tails → you pay $2,000
...and so on, doubling each time.

The catch: the moment you lose, the game ends
and you pay. You can walk away after any win.

🤔 At what point do you walk away?

Is this purely math? Purely psychology?
Or something else entirely?
